qa.question: What was the wiec?
qa.context: Concerning the early Polish tribes, geography contributed to long-standing traditions. The Polish tribes were internalized and organized around a unifying religious cult, governed by the wiec, an assembly of free tribesmen. Later, when safety required power to be consolidated, an elected prince was chosen to govern. The election privilege was usually limited to elites.
qa.encoded: The **wiec** was an assembly of free tribesmen that governed the early Polish tribes. Rooted in long-standing traditions shaped by geography, this gathering served as the central political institution through which tribal decisions were made. Each tribe was organized around a unifying religious cult, and the wiec provided the framework for collective rule. Essentially, it functioned as a democratic body where free men could voice opinions and help steer communal affairs. Only later, when external threats demanded more centralized authority, did the tribes begin electing princes to consolidate power — but even that privilege was zealously restricted to elites within the community.

qa.question: How can a researcher study a childs memory?
qa.context: Infants do not have the language ability to report on their memories and so verbal reports cannot be used to assess very young children’s memory. Throughout the years, however, researchers have adapted and developed a number of measures for assessing both infants’ recognition memory and their recall memory. Habituation and operant conditioning techniques have been used to assess infants’ recognition memory and the deferred and elicited imitation techniques have been used to assess infants’ recall memory.
qa.encoded: Researchers have developed several creative techniques to study memory in children, particularly since young kids often cannot verbalize what they remember. Even with infants who lack language skills, it is possible to assess memory through non-verbal behavioral methods. Since babies cannot report on their experiences directly, scientists rely on observable responses to understand what they retain. Essentially, two broad categories of memory are examined: recognition memory and recall memory. A proven way to measure recognition memory is through habituation — a baby is repeatedly shown a stimulus until interest fades, and then a new stimulus is introduced; renewed interest suggests the child recognizes the difference between old and new. Researchers also employ operant conditioning, such as training an infant to kick to move a mobile, and then testing days later whether the infant remembers the learned behavior. Concerning recall memory, deferred and elicited imitation are powerful tools — a child observes someone performing a specific action and is later given the opportunity to reproduce it, which demonstrates that the memory was stored and successfully retrieved. Having all of these methods at their disposal allows researchers to build a detailed, nuanced picture of how memory develops from the very earliest stages of life.

qa.question: Food goes in to the midgut after leaving where?
qa.context: Once food leaves the crop, it passes to the midgut (element 13 in numbered diagram), also known as the mesenteron, where the majority of digestion takes place. Microscopic projections from the midgut wall, called microvilli, increase the surface area of the wall and allow more nutrients to be absorbed; they tend to be close to the origin of the midgut. In some insects, the role of the microvilli and where they are located may vary. For example, specialized microvilli producing digestive enzymes may more likely be near the end of the midgut, and absorption near the origin or beginning of the midgut.:32
qa.encoded: Many insects have a digestive structure called the crop, and it is precisely from the crop that food passes into the midgut. The midgut, also known as the mesenteron, is where the majority of digestion actually takes place. Once food leaves the crop's storage area, it enters this region where microscopic projections called microvilli increase the surface area of the midgut wall, allowing more nutrients to be absorbed efficiently.

qa.question: What types of music did the Swingle Singers implement instrumental emulation for?
qa.context: In addition to singing words, some a cappella singers also emulate instrumentation by reproducing instrumental sounds with their vocal cords and mouth. One of the earliest 20th century practitioners of this method were The Mills Brothers whose early recordings of the 1930s clearly stated on the label that all instrumentation was done vocally. More recently, "Twilight Zone" by 2 Unlimited was sung a cappella to the instrumentation on the comedy television series Tompkins Square. Another famous example of emulating instrumentation instead of singing the words is the theme song for The New Addams Family series on Fox Family Channel (now ABC Family). Groups such as Vocal Sampling and Undivided emulate Latin rhythms a cappella. In the 1960s, the Swingle Singers used their voices to emulate musical instruments to Baroque and Classical music. Vocal artist Bobby McFerrin is famous for his instrumental emulation. A cappella group Naturally Seven recreates entire songs using vocal tones for every instrument.
qa.encoded: The Swingle Singers used their voices to emulate musical instruments for Baroque and Classical music. Active during the 1960s, they brought these revered genres to life through purely vocal means, transforming well-known orchestral and keyboard compositions into a captivating artistic mix.

qa.question: Who declined the ROK's request for tanks?
qa.context: In contrast, the ROK Army defenders were relatively unprepared and ill-equipped. In South to the Naktong, North to the Yalu (1961), R.E. Appleman reports the ROK forces' low combat readiness as of 25 June 1950. The ROK Army had 98,000 soldiers (65,000 combat, 33,000 support), no tanks (they had been requested from the U.S. military, but requests were denied), and a 22-piece air force comprising 12 liaison-type and 10 AT6 advanced-trainer airplanes. There were no large foreign military garrisons in Korea at the time of the invasion, but there were large U.S. garrisons and air forces in Japan.
qa.encoded: The U.S. military was responsible for declining the ROK Army's request for tanks prior to the Korean War. Records from R.E. Appleman's account in *South to the Naktong, North to the Yalu* confirm that South Korea had sought armored vehicles from the United States, but those requests were denied. As a result, when North Korea invaded on June 25, 1950, the ROK Army had no tanks whatsoever, leaving it at a severe disadvantage against the North Korean forces.

qa.question: What Frankish merchant was the ruler of the first Slav state in Central Europe?
qa.context: In the 7th century, the Frankish merchant Samo, who supported the Slavs fighting their Avar rulers, became the ruler of the first known Slav state in Central Europe, which, however, most probably did not outlive its founder and ruler. This provided the foundation for subsequent Slavic states to arise on the former territory of this realm with Carantania being the oldest of them. Very old also are the Principality of Nitra and the Moravian principality (see under Great Moravia). In this period, there existed central Slavic groups and states such as the Balaton Principality, but the subsequent expansion of the Magyars, as well as the Germanisation of Austria, separated the northern and southern Slavs. The First Bulgarian Empire was founded in 681, the Slavic language Old Bulgarian became the main and official of the empire in 864. Bulgaria was instrumental in the spread of Slavic literacy and Christianity to the rest of the Slavic world.
qa.encoded: The Frankish merchant Samo is recognized as the ruler of the first known Slav state in Central Europe, rising to power during the 7th century. Valiantly supporting the Slavs in their struggle against their Avar rulers, Samo earned their trust and became the leader of this early state. However, most historians believe his realm likely did not outlive its founder, though it laid the groundwork for subsequent Slavic states such as Carantania.

qa.question: Who gave up New Amsterdam to the English without a fight in 1664?

qa.encoded: Peter Stuyvesant, who served as Director-General of New Netherland, yielded New Amsterdam to the English without a fight in 1664. The English then renamed the city New York, honoring the Duke of York.

qa.question: What did the later designs of capacitors used in radio tuning circuits no longer have?
qa.context: Most types of capacitor include a dielectric spacer, which increases their capacitance. These dielectrics are most often insulators. However, low capacitance devices are available with a vacuum between their plates, which allows extremely high voltage operation and low losses. Variable capacitors with their plates open to the atmosphere were commonly used in radio tuning circuits. Later designs use polymer foil dielectric between the moving and stationary plates, with no significant air space between them.
qa.encoded: Quite notably, the later designs of capacitors used in radio tuning circuits no longer had a significant air space between their plates. These updated versions used polymer foil dielectric placed directly between the moving and stationary plates, eliminating the open-air gap that earlier variable capacitors had relied upon when their plates were exposed to the atmosphere.

qa.question: Who wrote the supplemental material added to the Lectures book in 2005?
qa.context: The Feynman Lectures on Physics is perhaps his most accessible work for anyone with an interest in physics, compiled from lectures to Caltech undergraduates in 1961–64. As news of the lectures' lucidity grew, professional physicists and graduate students began to drop in to listen. Co-authors Robert B. Leighton and Matthew Sands, colleagues of Feynman, edited and illustrated them into book form. The work has endured and is useful to this day. They were edited and supplemented in 2005 with "Feynman's Tips on Physics: A Problem-Solving Supplement to the Feynman Lectures on Physics" by Michael Gottlieb and Ralph Leighton (Robert Leighton's son), with support from Kip Thorne and other physicists.
qa.encoded: The supplemental material added to the Feynman Lectures in 2005 was written by Michael Gottlieb and Ralph Leighton. Ralph is the son of Robert B. Leighton, who was one of the original co-authors of the Lectures. They received support from Kip Thorne and other physicists in creating this companion volume, titled *Feynman's Tips on Physics: A Problem-Solving Supplement to the Feynman Lectures on Physics*.

qa.question: What is the advantage of a coiled-coil filament over a straight filament?
qa.context: To improve the efficiency of the lamp, the filament usually consists of multiple coils of coiled fine wire, also known as a 'coiled coil'. For a 60-watt 120-volt lamp, the uncoiled length of the tungsten filament is usually 22.8 inches (580 mm), and the filament diameter is 0.0018 inches (0.046 mm). The advantage of the coiled coil is that evaporation of the tungsten filament is at the rate of a tungsten cylinder having a diameter equal to that of the coiled coil. The coiled-coil filament evaporates more slowly than a straight filament of the same surface area and light-emitting power. As a result, the filament can then run hotter, which results in a more efficient light source, while reducing the evaporation so that the filament will last longer than a straight filament at the same temperature.
qa.encoded: The coiled-coil filament provides a significant advantage over a straight filament in terms of both efficiency and longevity. The key reason is that tungsten evaporation from a coiled-coil occurs at the rate of a cylinder whose diameter matches that of the overall coil, rather than at the rate of the thin wire's actual surface area. This effectively means the coiled-coil filament evaporates much more slowly than a straight filament producing the same amount of light. Thanks to this reduced evaporation rate, the filament can be operated at a higher temperature, which produces more light per watt of electricity consumed. The hotter operating temperature directly translates to greater luminous efficiency. The filament also enjoys a longer operational lifespan compared to a straight filament running at the same temperature, since less tungsten is lost over time. Thus, the coiled-coil design achieves the best of both worlds — brighter, more efficient light output and improved durability.

qa.question: In 1887 the US Government wanted to undo Bell's patent due to fraud and what?
qa.context: On January 13, 1887, the U,S. Government moved to annul the patent issued to Bell on the grounds of fraud and misrepresentation. After a series of decisions and reversals, the Bell company won a decision in the Supreme Court, though a couple of the original claims from the lower court cases were left undecided. By the time that the trial wound its way through nine years of legal battles, the U.S. prosecuting attorney had died and the two Bell patents (No. 174,465 dated March 7, 1876 and No. 186,787 dated January 30, 1877) were no longer in effect, although the presiding judges agreed to continue the proceedings due to the case's importance as a "precedent". With a change in administration and charges of conflict of interest (on both sides) arising from the original trial, the US Attorney General dropped the lawsuit on November 30, 1897 leaving several issues undecided on the merits.
qa.encoded: In 1887, the U.S. Government moved to annul Alexander Graham Bell's telephone patent on the grounds of fraud and **misrepresentation**. The case dragged on for nine years through a series of decisions and reversals, eventually reaching the Supreme Court, where the Bell company secured a favorable ruling—though some original claims from lower court cases were left undecided. By that point, both Bell patents had expired, yet the presiding judges allowed proceedings to continue given the case's significance as a legal precedent. Ultimately, after a change in administration and accusations of conflict of interest on both sides, the U.S. Attorney General chose to drop the lawsuit on November 30, 1897, leaving several crucial questions unresolved on their merits.

qa.question: In addition to conversion, what other reason is given for the trend in Christianity's followers?
qa.context: According to 2012 Pew Research Center survey if current trends continue, Christianity will remains the world's largest religion by year 2050. By 2050, the Christian population is expected to exceed 3 billion. While Muslims have an average of 3.1 children per woman—the highest rate of all religious groups. Christians are second, with 2.7 children per woman. High birth rates and conversion were cited as the reason for the Christian population growths. A 2015 study found that approximately 10.2 million Muslim converted to Christianity. Christianity is growing in Africa, Asia, Latin America, Muslim world, and Oceania.
qa.encoded: The 2012 Pew Research Center survey points to high birth rates as a significant contributing factor to the growth in Christianity's followers, alongside conversion. Notably, Christian women average 2.7 children per woman, which is the second-highest fertility rate among all religious groups, just behind Muslims at 3.1 children per woman. These elevated birth rates, combined with ongoing conversions — including approximately 10.2 million Muslims who converted to Christianity according to a 2015 study — are projected to push the global Christian population beyond 3 billion by the year 2050.

qa.question: In addition to cannon fire, what did Franklin suggest to act as Parisians' alarm clock?
qa.context: During his time as an American envoy to France, Benjamin Franklin, publisher of the old English proverb, "Early to bed, and early to rise, makes a man healthy, wealthy and wise", anonymously published a letter suggesting that Parisians economize on candles by rising earlier to use morning sunlight. This 1784 satire proposed taxing shutters, rationing candles, and waking the public by ringing church bells and firing cannons at sunrise. Despite common misconception, Franklin did not actually propose DST; 18th-century Europe did not even keep precise schedules. However, this soon changed as rail and communication networks came to require a standardization of time unknown in Franklin's day.
qa.encoded: Ultimately, Franklin proposed that church bells be rung at sunrise to serve as an alarm for the citizens of Paris. This suggestion appeared in his 1784 satirical letter, where he humorously recommended a combination of measures—including taxing window shutters and rationing candles—to encourage Parisians to wake earlier and take advantage of natural morning sunlight rather than burning candles needlessly.

qa.question: In what state did Whitehead believe religion began?
qa.context: However, while Whitehead saw religion as beginning in solitariness, he also saw religion as necessarily expanding beyond the individual. In keeping with his process metaphysics in which relations are primary, he wrote that religion necessitates the realization of "the value of the objective world which is a community derivative from the interrelations of its component individuals." In other words, the universe is a community which makes itself whole through the relatedness of each individual entity to all the others – meaning and value do not exist for the individual alone, but only in the context of the universal community. Whitehead writes further that each entity "can find no such value till it has merged its individual claim with that of the objective universe. Religion is world-loyalty. The spirit at once surrenders itself to this universal claim and appropriates it for itself." In this way the individual and universal/social aspects of religion are mutually dependent.
qa.encoded: According to Whitehead, religion begins in a state of solitariness — a deeply personal and individual experience. He maintained that the religious impulse first arises within one's own solitary contemplation and awareness. However, Whitehead also argued that religion must necessarily grow beyond this private origin, as meaning and value only emerge through the interconnected community of all beings. What he ultimately described as "world-loyalty" represents the fulfillment of this expansion, where the individual spirit surrenders to and embraces the universal claim of the objective universe.

qa.question: How much did the percentage of free people in the South change from 1782 to 1810?
qa.context: After the American Revolutionary War, the number and proportion of free people of color increased markedly in the North and the South as slaves were freed. Most northern states abolished slavery, sometimes, like New York, in programs of gradual emancipation that took more than two decades to be completed. The last slaves in New York were not freed until 1827. In connection with the Second Great Awakening, Quaker and Methodist preachers in the South urged slaveholders to free their slaves. Revolutionary ideals led many men to free their slaves, some by deed and others by will, so that from 1782 to 1810, the percentage of free people of color rose from less than one percent to nearly 10 percent of blacks in the South.
qa.encoded: Nine percentage points represents the approximate change in the proportion of free people of color in the South during that period. Specifically, the percentage rose from less than one percent in 1782 to nearly 10 percent by 1810. This dramatic shift was largely driven by Revolutionary ideals and the influence of religious movements, particularly Quaker and Methodist preachers, who urged slaveholders to manumit their slaves.

qa.question: What kind of institutions do countries like Germany and Switzerland consider institutes of technology to be?
qa.context: In several countries, like Germany, the Netherlands, Switzerland and Turkey, institutes of technology and polytechnics are institutions of higher education, and have been accredited to award academic degrees and doctorates. Famous examples are the Istanbul Technical University, ETH Zurich, İYTE, Delft University of Technology and RWTH Aachen, all considered universities.[citation needed]
qa.encoded: Countries like Germany, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and Turkey consider institutes of technology and polytechnics to be institutions of higher education. These institutions are fully accredited to award academic degrees and even doctorates. Key examples such as ETH Zurich, Delft University of Technology, and RWTH Aachen are all regarded as full universities in their respective nations.

qa.question: Other than the bit rate and the difficulty of the signal, what can also affect the quality of an MP3 file?
qa.context: Besides the bit rate of an encoded piece of audio, the quality of MP3 files also depends on the quality of the encoder itself, and the difficulty of the signal being encoded. As the MP3 standard allows quite a bit of freedom with encoding algorithms, different encoders may feature quite different quality, even with identical bit rates. As an example, in a public listening test featuring two different MP3 encoders at about 128 kbit/s, one scored 3.66 on a 1–5 scale, while the other scored only 2.22.
qa.encoded: Not only does the bit rate and signal difficulty matter, but the quality of the encoder itself can significantly affect the resulting MP3 file's quality. In practice, the MP3 standard permits considerable freedom in how encoding algorithms are designed and implemented. Notable differences in output quality can exist between two encoders operating at the same bit rate — for instance, in a public listening test at approximately 128 kbit/s, one encoder scored 3.66 out of 5 while another managed only 2.22. Encoder choice is therefore a critical factor that listeners and audio professionals should carefully consider when producing or distributing MP3 audio.

qa.question: What can be shorter than 1/4 wavelength long?
qa.context: In some cases this is done in a more extreme manner, not simply to cancel a small amount of residual reactance, but to resonate an antenna whose resonance frequency is quite different from the intended frequency of operation. For instance, a "whip antenna" can be made significantly shorter than 1/4 wavelength long, for practical reasons, and then resonated using a so-called loading coil. This physically large inductor at the base of the antenna has an inductive reactance which is the opposite of the capacitative reactance that such a vertical antenna has at the desired operating frequency. The result is a pure resistance seen at feedpoint of the loading coil; unfortunately that resistance is somewhat lower than would be desired to match commercial coax.[citation needed]
qa.encoded: A whip antenna can be made significantly shorter than one-quarter wavelength long for practical reasons. A loading coil then resonates this antenna at its desired frequency.

qa.question: What was largely ignored and upset many of Sadat's polidy?
qa.context: In 1975, Sadat shifted Nasser's economic policies and sought to use his popularity to reduce government regulations and encourage foreign investment through his program of Infitah. Through this policy, incentives such as reduced taxes and import tariffs attracted some investors, but investments were mainly directed at low risk and profitable ventures like tourism and construction, abandoning Egypt's infant industries. Even though Sadat's policy was intended to modernise Egypt and assist the middle class, it mainly benefited the higher class, and, because of the elimination of subsidies on basic foodstuffs, led to the 1977 Egyptian Bread Riots.
qa.encoded: Egypt's infant industries were largely ignored under Sadat's policy. Instead of supporting domestic manufacturing and industrial development, investments under the Infitah program were primarily directed toward low-risk, profitable ventures like tourism and construction. The policy was meant to modernize Egypt and help the middle class, but it mainly benefited the wealthy elite. Additionally, the removal of subsidies on basic foodstuffs deeply upset ordinary citizens, ultimately sparking the 1977 Egyptian Bread Riots.

qa.question: What nickname was given to IBM?
qa.context: The company originated in 1911 as the Computing-Tabulating-Recording Company (CTR) through the consolidation of The Tabulating Machine Company, the International Time Recording Company, the Computing Scale Company and the Bundy Manufacturing Company. CTR was renamed "International Business Machines" in 1924, a name which Thomas J. Watson first used for a CTR Canadian subsidiary. The initialism IBM followed. Securities analysts nicknamed the company Big Blue for its size and common use of the color in products, packaging and its logo.
qa.encoded: Very notably, securities analysts gave IBM the nickname "Big Blue" for its size and widespread use of blue in products and packaging.

qa.question: Where are regulations codified into?
qa.context: Federal law originates with the Constitution, which gives Congress the power to enact statutes for certain limited purposes like regulating interstate commerce. The United States Code is the official compilation and codification of the general and permanent federal statutes. Many statutes give executive branch agencies the power to create regulations, which are published in the Federal Register and codified into the Code of Federal Regulations. Regulations generally also carry the force of law under the Chevron doctrine. Many lawsuits turn on the meaning of a federal statute or regulation, and judicial interpretations of such meaning carry legal force under the principle of stare decisis.
qa.encoded: Regulations are codified into the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR). This comprehensive compilation organizes all general and permanent rules that have been established by the executive branch agencies of the federal government. Codification into the CFR ensures these rules are systematically arranged by subject matter and remain accessible to the public, carrying the force of law.

qa.question: Have transfer fees been increasing over the past years?
qa.context: The record transfer fee for a Premier League player has risen steadily over the lifetime of the competition. Prior to the start of the first Premier League season Alan Shearer became the first British player to command a transfer fee of more than £3 million. The record rose steadily in the Premier League's first few seasons, until Alan Shearer made a record breaking £15 million move to Newcastle United in 1996. The three highest transfer in the sport's history had a Premier League club on the selling end, with Tottenham Hotspur selling Gareth Bale to Real Madrid for £85 million in 2013, Manchester United's sale of Cristiano Ronaldo to Real Madrid for £80 million in 2009, and Liverpool selling Luis Suárez to Barcelona for £75 million in 2014.
qa.encoded: Yes, transfer fees have been increasing quite dramatically over the years in the Premier League. The record transfer fee has risen steadily since the competition began. Alan Shearer set early benchmarks, first breaking the £3 million barrier and later making a record £15 million move to Newcastle United in 1996. Since then, the figures have soared even higher, with transfers like Gareth Bale's £85 million move to Real Madrid in 2013, Cristiano Ronaldo's £80 million sale in 2009, and Luis Suárez's £75 million transfer to Barcelona in 2014. These examples clearly illustrate a strong upward trend in player valuations over time.

qa.question: What ethnicity is growing the quickest in New York City?
qa.context: The city's population in 2010 was 44% white (33.3% non-Hispanic white), 25.5% black (23% non-Hispanic black), 0.7% Native American, and 12.7% Asian. Hispanics of any race represented 28.6% of the population, while Asians constituted the fastest-growing segment of the city's population between 2000 and 2010; the non-Hispanic white population declined 3 percent, the smallest recorded decline in decades; and for the first time since the Civil War, the number of blacks declined over a decade.
qa.encoded: Asians were the fastest-growing ethnic group in New York City between 2000 and 2010. Growth among the Asian community outpaced all other demographic shifts during that decade — while the non-Hispanic white population declined by about 3 percent and the black population decreased for the first time since the Civil War, the Asian segment expanded significantly.

qa.question: Who directed The Terminator?
qa.context: Schwarzenegger's breakthrough film was the sword-and-sorcery epic Conan the Barbarian in 1982, which was a box-office hit. This was followed by a sequel, Conan the Destroyer, in 1984, although it was not as successful as its predecessor. In 1983, Schwarzenegger starred in the promotional video, Carnival in Rio. In 1984, he made his first appearance as the eponymous character, and what some would say was his acting career's signature role, in James Cameron's science fiction thriller film The Terminator. Following this, Schwarzenegger made Red Sonja in 1985.
qa.encoded: The Terminator was directed by James Cameron. Notably, the 1984 science fiction thriller went on to become one of Schwarzenegger's most iconic films, with many considering the role of the Terminator to be his signature performance throughout his acting career.

qa.question: the term 'house music' came from the labeling of records at what store?
qa.context: Chip E.'s 1985 recording "It's House" may also have helped to define this new form of electronic music. However, Chip E. himself lends credence to the Knuckles association, claiming the name came from methods of labeling records at the Importes Etc. record store, where he worked in the early 1980s: bins of music that DJ Knuckles played at the Warehouse nightclub were labelled in the store "As Heard At The Warehouse", which was shortened to simply "House". Patrons later asked for new music for the bins, which Chip E. implies was a demand the shop tried to meet by stocking newer local club hits.
qa.encoded: The term "house music" originated from the labeling of records at **Importes Etc.**, a record store in Chicago. This store employed Chip E. in the early 1980s, and bins of music that DJ Frankie Knuckles played at the Warehouse nightclub were labeled "As Heard At The Warehouse." Very quickly, this phrase was shortened to simply "House." The store's patrons then began requesting new music for those bins, effectively driving demand for the emerging local electronic club sound that would become known as house music.

qa.question: According to Popper, what is not grounds for doubt?
qa.context: To Popper, who was an anti-justificationist, traditional philosophy is misled by the false principle of sufficient reason. He thinks that no assumption can ever be or needs ever to be justified, so a lack of justification is not a justification for doubt. Instead, theories should be tested and scrutinised. It is not the goal to bless theories with claims of certainty or justification, but to eliminate errors in them. He writes, "there are no such things as good positive reasons; nor do we need such things [...] But [philosophers] obviously cannot quite bring [themselves] to believe that this is my opinion, let alone that it is right" (The Philosophy of Karl Popper, p. 1043)
qa.encoded: According to Popper, a lack of justification is not grounds for doubt. Put simply, as an anti-justificationist, he held that no assumption can ever be or needs ever to be justified, and so the absence of justification cannot itself serve as a reason for skepticism. Quite central to his position is the rejection of what he called the "false principle of sufficient reason," which mistakenly demands that all beliefs be positively justified. The proper goal, in Popper's view, is never to bestow certainty or justification upon theories but rather to rigorously test and scrutinize them in order to eliminate errors.

qa.question: In what year was the Schwarzenegger Institute for State and Global Policy founded?
qa.context: In 2012, Schwarzenegger helped to found the Schwarzenegger Institute for State and Global Policy, which is a part of the USC Sol Price School of Public Policy at the University of Southern California. The Institute's mission is to "[advance] post-partisanship, where leaders put people over political parties and work together to find the best ideas and solutions to benefit the people they serve," and to "seek to influence public policy and public debate in finding solutions to the serious challenges we face." Schwarzenegger serves as chairman of the Institute.
qa.encoded: The Schwarzenegger Institute for State and Global Policy was founded in 2012. Upon its establishment, Arnold Schwarzenegger took on the role of chairman, with the Institute becoming part of the USC Sol Price School of Public Policy at the University of Southern California. Its stated mission centers on advancing post-partisanship and finding collaborative solutions to major policy challenges.

qa.question: What was a consequence of the large loan defaults and MBS losses in 2007?
qa.context: These institutions, as well as certain regulated banks, had also assumed significant debt burdens while providing the loans described above and did not have a financial cushion sufficient to absorb large loan defaults or MBS losses. These losses impacted the ability of financial institutions to lend, slowing economic activity. Concerns regarding the stability of key financial institutions drove central banks to provide funds to encourage lending and restore faith in the commercial paper markets, which are integral to funding business operations. Governments also bailed out key financial institutions and implemented economic stimulus programs, assuming significant additional financial commitments.
qa.encoded: The large loan defaults and MBS losses profoundly impacted the ability of financial institutions to lend, progressively slowing economic activity. These institutions had previously taken on significant debt burdens while providing loans, and they did not possess a sufficient financial cushion to absorb such losses. Concerns about the stability of key financial institutions prompted central banks to provide funds to encourage lending and to restore faith in the commercial paper markets — pivotal to funding business operations. In response, governments pursued bailouts of key financial institutions and implemented economic stimulus programs, placing significant additional financial commitments on the public sector.

qa.question: How many islands are along the rugged coastline of Zhejiang?
qa.context: There are over three thousand islands along the rugged coastline of Zhejiang. The largest, Zhoushan Island, is Mainland China's third largest island, after Hainan and Chongming. There are also many bays, of which Hangzhou Bay is the largest.  Zhejiang has a humid subtropical climate with four distinct seasons. Spring starts in March and is rainy with changeable weather. Summer, from June to September is long, hot, rainy, and humid. Fall is generally dry, warm and sunny. Winters are short but cold except in the far south. Average annual temperature is around 15 to 19 °C (59 to 66 °F), average January temperature is around 2 to 8 °C (36 to 46 °F) and average July temperature is around 27 to 30 °C (81 to 86 °F). Annual precipitation is about 1,000 to 1,900 mm (39 to 75 in). There is plenty of rainfall in early summer, and by late summer Zhejiang is directly threatened by typhoons forming in the Pacific.
qa.encoded: There are over three thousand islands along the rugged coastline of Zhejiang. The largest of these is Zhoushan Island, which also ranks as Mainland China's third largest island, after Hainan and Chongming. These islands, combined with numerous bays — Hangzhou Bay being the most prominent — create a dramatic coastal geography that yearly faces the threat of powerful Pacific typhoons during late summer months.

qa.question: What modern building material did Viollet-le-Duc teach reform Gothic designers to work with?
qa.context: In France, simultaneously, the towering figure of the Gothic Revival was Eugène Viollet-le-Duc, who outdid historical Gothic constructions to create a Gothic as it ought to have been, notably at the fortified city of Carcassonne in the south of France and in some richly fortified keeps for industrial magnates. Viollet-le-Duc compiled and coordinated an Encyclopédie médiévale that was a rich repertory his contemporaries mined for architectural details. He effected vigorous restoration of crumbling detail of French cathedrals, including the Abbey of Saint-Denis and famously at Notre Dame de Paris, where many of whose most "Gothic" gargoyles are Viollet-le-Duc's. He taught a generation of reform-Gothic designers and showed how to apply Gothic style to modern structural materials, especially cast iron.
qa.encoded: Viollet-le-Duc taught reform-Gothic designers to work with cast iron, a modern structural material that was becoming widely available during the industrial era. He showed how Gothic architectural principles could be adapted to this material, effectively bridging medieval design aesthetics and contemporary building technology. Cast iron allowed for structural innovations that traditional stone could not easily achieve, giving designers new creative possibilities while still honoring the Gothic tradition.

qa.question: What part of the iPod Nano was the cause of the overheating issue?
qa.context: The first generation iPod Nano may overheat and pose a health and safety risk. Affected iPod Nanos were sold between September 2005 and December 2006. This is due to a flawed battery used by Apple from a single battery manufacturer. Apple recommended that owners of affected iPod Nanos stop using them. Under an Apple product replacement program, affected Nanos were replaced with current generation Nanos free of charge.
qa.encoded: The overheating issue with the first generation iPod Nano was caused by a defective battery. Apple sourced the flawed battery cells from a single manufacturer. Known to overheat, these batteries posed a serious health and safety risk. Devices sold between September 2005 and December 2006 were affected by this issue. Apple ultimately recommended that owners stop using affected Nanos and offered free replacements with current generation models.

qa.question: What did the European Commission review
qa.context: Hinkley Point C nuclear power station is a project to construct a 3,200 MW two reactor nuclear power station. On 18 October 2010, the British government announced that Hinkley Point – already the site of the disused Hinkley Point A and the still operational Hinkley Point B power stations – was one of the eight sites it considered suitable for future nuclear power stations. NNB Generation Company, a subsidiary of EDF, submitted an application for development consent to the Infrastructure Planning Commission on 31 October 2011. A protest group, Stop Hinkley, was formed to campaign for the closure of Hinkley Point B and oppose any expansion at the Hinkley Point site. In December 2013, the European Commission opened an investigation to assess whether the project breaks state-aid rules. On 8 October 2014 it was announced that the European Commission has approved the project, with an overwhelming majority and only four commissioners voting against the decision.
qa.encoded: Nuclear power projects in the EU must comply with state-aid regulations, and this is exactly what the European Commission reviewed regarding Hinkley Point C. Opening a formal investigation in December 2013, the Commission examined whether the financial support arrangements provided by the British government for the project constituted illegal state aid under EU rules. On 8 October 2014, the Commission concluded its review and approved the project with an overwhelming majority of commissioners backing the decision. Notably, only four commissioners voted against the approval, confirming that the arrangements for Hinkley Point C were deemed compatible with EU state-aid regulations.

qa.question: How many members are there in the Swazi House of Assembly?
qa.context: The Swazi bicameral Parliament or Libandla consists of the Senate (30 seats; 10 members appointed by the House of Assembly and 20 appointed by the monarch; to serve five-year terms) and the House of Assembly (65 seats; 10 members appointed by the monarch and 55 elected by popular vote; to serve five-year terms). The elections are held every five years after dissolution of parliament by the king. The last elections were held on 20 September 2013. The balloting is done on a non-party basis in all categories. All election procedures are overseen by the elections and boundaries commission.
qa.encoded: The Swazi House of Assembly has 65 members. Of these, 55 are elected by popular vote on a non-party basis, while the remaining 10 are appointed directly by the monarch. All members serve five-year terms, with elections held following each dissolution of parliament by the king. This body, together with the 30-member Senate, forms the bicameral Swazi Parliament known as the Libandla.

qa.question: When did New York City annex part of Pelham?
qa.context: The consolidation of the Bronx into New York City proceeded in two stages. In 1873, the state legislature annexed Kingsbridge, West Farms and Morrisania to New York, effective in 1874; the three towns were abolished in the process. In 1895, three years before New York's consolidation with Brooklyn, Queens and Staten Island, the whole of the territory east of the Bronx River, including the Town of Westchester (which had voted in 1894 against consolidation) and portions of Eastchester and Pelham, were annexed to the city. City Island, a nautical community, voted to join the city in 1896.
qa.encoded: In 1895, portions of Pelham were annexed to New York City. This was part of a broader consolidation effort that brought all territory east of the Bronx River into the city. The Town of Westchester and portions of Eastchester were also included in this same annexation. Interestingly, the Town of Westchester had actually voted against consolidation in 1894, but was annexed regardless. Despite such local opposition, the state legislature moved forward and completed the annexation that year.

qa.question: Which of Melbourne's suburbs have seen significant brownfields redevelopment in recent years?
qa.context: Melbourne is experiencing high population growth, generating high demand for housing. This housing boom has increased house prices and rents, as well as the availability of all types of housing. Subdivision regularly occurs in the outer areas of Melbourne, with numerous developers offering house and land packages. However, after 10 years[when?] of planning policies to encourage medium-density and high-density development in existing areas with greater access to public transport and other services, Melbourne's middle and outer-ring suburbs have seen significant brownfields redevelopment.
qa.encoded: Based on planning policies implemented over roughly the past decade encouraging medium-density and high-density development, Melbourne's middle and outer-ring suburbs have experienced significant brownfields redevelopment. These policies targeted existing areas with greater access to public transport and other services, shifting growth away from simple outward subdivision in the city's outer fringes toward redeveloping previously used land closer in.

qa.question: What is responsible for electrostatic interaction between electric charges?
qa.context: In particle physics, this inequality permits a qualitative understanding of virtual particles which carry momentum, exchange by which and with real particles, is responsible for the creation of all known fundamental forces (more accurately known as fundamental interactions). Virtual photons (which are simply lowest quantum mechanical energy state of photons) are also responsible for electrostatic interaction between electric charges (which results in Coulomb law), for spontaneous radiative decay of exited atomic and nuclear states, for the Casimir force, for van der Waals bond forces and some other observable phenomena.
qa.encoded: Virtual photons are responsible for the electrostatic interaction between electric charges. In particle physics, these virtual photons represent the lowest quantum mechanical energy state of photons. They act as mediators of the electromagnetic force by facilitating momentum exchange between charged particles. This exchange mechanism is what gives rise to the familiar Coulomb's law governing how charges attract or repel one another. The underlying principle that permits virtual photons to exist, even briefly, is the energy-time uncertainty relation from quantum mechanics. Consequently, virtual photons serve a foundational role in our modern understanding of how electric charges interact at the most fundamental level.

qa.question: Who was the informal leader of the slaves to whom there is a statue dedicated beside City Hall in New Haven?
qa.context: New Haven was home to one of the important early events in the burgeoning anti-slavery movement when, in 1839, the trial of mutineering Mende tribesmen being transported as slaves on the Spanish slaveship Amistad was held in New Haven's United States District Court. There is a statue of Joseph Cinqué, the informal leader of the slaves, beside City Hall. See "Museums" below for more information. Abraham Lincoln delivered a speech on slavery in New Haven in 1860, shortly before he secured the Republican nomination for President.
qa.encoded: Joseph Cinqué was the informal leader of the slaves aboard the Amistad. His quest for freedom alongside his fellow Mende tribesmen became a landmark moment in the anti-slavery movement after their 1839 trial in New Haven's United States District Court. A statue honoring Cinqué stands beside City Hall in New Haven today.

qa.question: How many people died in the Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire?

In 1911, the Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire, the city's worst industrial disaster, took the lives of 146 garment workers and spurred the growth of the International Ladies' Garment Workers' Union and major improvements in factory safety standards.

qa.question: In what year were twice as many glacial earthquakes seen than in any other year in Greenland?
qa.context: In glaciated areas where the glacier moves faster than one km per year, glacial earthquakes occur. These are large scale temblors that have seismic magnitudes as high as 6.1. The number of glacial earthquakes in Greenland peaks every year in July, August and September and is increasing over time. In a study using data from January 1993 through October 2005, more events were detected every year since 2002, and twice as many events were recorded in 2005 as there were in any other year. This increase in the numbers of glacial earthquakes in Greenland may be a response to global warming.
qa.encoded: Notably, 2005 saw twice as many glacial earthquakes in Greenland compared to any other year in the study. Researchers analyzing data from January 1993 through October 2005 found that the number of detected events had been climbing since 2002, but 2005 stood out dramatically with double the glacial earthquake activity of any previous year. This remarkable surge has been linked to the possibility that increasing glacial earthquakes may be a response to global warming.

qa.question: How are the terms Hispanic and Latino not specific?
qa.context: The term "Hispanic" as an ethnonym emerged in the 20th century with the rise of migration of laborers from the Spanish-speaking countries of Latin America to the United States. Today, the word "Latino" is often used as a synonym for "Hispanic". The definitions of both terms are non-race specific, and include people who consider themselves to be of distinct races (Black, White, Amerindian, Asian, and mixed groups). However, there is a common misconception in the US that Hispanic/Latino is a race or sometimes even that national origins such as Mexican, Cuban, Colombian, Salvadoran, etc. are races. In contrast to "Latino" or "Hispanic", "Anglo" refers to non-Hispanic White Americans or non-Hispanic European Americans, most of whom speak the English language but are not necessarily of English descent.
qa.encoded: Notably, the definitions of both "Hispanic" and "Latino" are non-race specific, meaning they do not correspond to any single racial category. Individuals who identify with either term may consider themselves to be of entirely distinct races, including Black, White, Amerindian, Asian, or mixed racial groups. Neither label narrows identification down to one particular race or physical characteristic. This is often misunderstood in the United States, where many people mistakenly believe that Hispanic or Latino is itself a race, or even that specific national origins such as Mexican, Cuban, Colombian, or Salvadoran constitute separate races. Hence, both terms function purely as ethnic and cultural descriptors tied to Spanish-speaking heritage rather than as racial classifications.

qa.question: What organization operates the Delhi Metro system?
qa.context: Delhi Metro is being built and operated by the Delhi Metro Rail Corporation Limited (DMRC), a state-owned company with equal equity participation from Government of India and Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi. However, the organisation is under administrative control of Ministry of Urban Development, Government of India. Besides construction and operation of Delhi metro, DMRC is also involved in the planning and implementation of metro rail, monorail and high-speed rail projects in India and providing consultancy services to other metro projects in the country as well as abroad. The Delhi Metro project was spearheaded by Padma Vibhushan E. Sreedharan, the Managing Director of DMRC and popularly known as the "Metro Man" of India. He famously resigned from DMRC, taking moral responsibility for a metro bridge collapse which took five lives. Sreedharan was awarded with the prestigious Legion of Honour by the French Government for his contribution to Delhi Metro.
qa.encoded: The Delhi Metro system is operated by the Delhi Metro Rail Corporation Limited, commonly known as DMRC. It is a state-owned company established with equal equity participation from the Government of India and the Government of the National Capital Territory of Delhi. The organization functions under the administrative control of the Ministry of Urban Development, Government of India. Beyond operating the metro, DMRC also provides consultancy services for metro rail and other transit projects both domestically and internationally. Notably, the project was spearheaded by E. Sreedharan, celebrated as the "Metro Man" of India, who earned wide recognition for his extraordinary leadership in bringing the system to life.

qa.question: What was the warning signal called that warned the sender that there was impending overflow?
qa.context: When a Teletype 33 ASR equipped with the automatic paper tape reader received a Control-S (XOFF, an abbreviation for transmit off), it caused the tape reader to stop; receiving Control-Q (XON, "transmit on") caused the tape reader to resume. This technique became adopted by several early computer operating systems as a "handshaking" signal warning a sender to stop transmission because of impending overflow; it persists to this day in many systems as a manual output control technique. On some systems Control-S retains its meaning but Control-Q is replaced by a second Control-S to resume output. The 33 ASR also could be configured to employ Control-R (DC2) and Control-T (DC4) to start and stop the tape punch; on some units equipped with this function, the corresponding control character lettering on the keycap above the letter was TAPE and TAPE respectively.
qa.encoded: The technique used to warn a sender about impending overflow was known as a "handshaking" signal. Having originated on the Teletype 33 ASR, this method utilized Control-S (XOFF) to pause transmission and Control-Q (XON) to resume it. Early computer operating systems widely adopted this flow control approach. Yet this mechanism persists to this day in many systems as a manual output control technique.

qa.question: The idea of the image of God allows man to do what?
qa.context: Concerning humanity as the image of Christ, English Dominican spirituality concentrated on the moral implications of image-bearing rather than the philosophical foundations of the imago Dei. The idea of the "image of God" demonstrates both the ability of man to move toward God (as partakers in Christ's redeeming sacrifice), and that, on some level, man is always an image of God. As their love and knowledge of God grows and is sanctified by faith and experience, the image of God within man becomes ever more bright and clear.
qa.encoded: The idea of the image of God demonstrates man's ability to move toward God, serving as partakers in Christ's redeeming sacrifice. Kindled by faith and experience, man's love and knowledge of God grows, causing the image of God within man to become ever more bright and clear. It also reveals that, on some fundamental level, man is always an image of God regardless of where he stands on that spiritual journey.

qa.question: At what temperature does the metal become malleable?
qa.context: Zinc is a bluish-white, lustrous, diamagnetic metal, though most common commercial grades of the metal have a dull finish. It is somewhat less dense than iron and has a hexagonal crystal structure, with a distorted form of hexagonal close packing, in which each atom has six nearest neighbors (at 265.9 pm) in its own plane and six others at a greater distance of 290.6 pm. The metal is hard and brittle at most temperatures but becomes malleable between 100 and 150 °C. Above 210 °C, the metal becomes brittle again and can be pulverized by beating. Zinc is a fair conductor of electricity. For a metal, zinc has relatively low melting (419.5 °C) and boiling points (907 °C). Its melting point is the lowest of all the transition metals aside from mercury and cadmium.
qa.encoded: According to the information given, zinc becomes malleable between 100 and 150 degrees Celsius. Outside this temperature range, the metal tends to be hard and brittle.

qa.question: In kilometers, what is the distance from Plymouth to Exeter?
qa.context: Plymouth (i/ˈplɪməθ/) is a city on the south coast of Devon, England, about 37 miles (60 km) south-west of Exeter and 190 miles (310 km) west-south-west of London, between the mouths of the rivers Plym to the east and Tamar to the west where they join Plymouth Sound to form the boundary with Cornwall.
qa.encoded: The distance from Plymouth to Exeter is approximately 60 kilometres (km). Going by commonly referenced geographical data, this is equivalent to roughly 37 miles, with Plymouth situated to the south-west of Exeter along the Devon coast.

qa.question: When did people first start arriving in the European continent?
qa.context: The most important demographic influence in the modern Portuguese seems to be the oldest one; current interpretation of Y-chromosome and mtDNA data suggests that the Portuguese have their origin in Paleolithic peoples that began arriving to the European continent around 45,000 years ago. All subsequent migrations did leave an impact, genetically and culturally, but the main population source of the Portuguese is still Paleolithic. Genetic studies show Portuguese populations not to be significantly different from other European populations.
qa.encoded: People first started arriving on the European continent around 45,000 years ago, based on current interpretations of Y-chromosome and mtDNA data. These early Paleolithic settlers are considered the most significant demographic source for modern Portuguese populations. While quite a number of subsequent migrations left both genetic and cultural impacts, the primary ancestral origin traces back to these Paleolithic arrivals.

qa.question: What is the publication that is dedicated to the analysis of current events and public policy?

qa.encoded: Politics & Policy is the publication dedicated to the analysis of current events and public policy.
